data.description:

  • On our checks, Nelnet has a valuation score of 0 out of 6, which we will break down using several common valuation approaches, before finishing with a way of looking at value that can help tie all these methods together.

Approach 1: Nelnet Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company can generate above the return that equity investors typically require. It starts with what shareholders have invested, compares that to the earnings the business can sustainably generate, and then values the stream of any excess profit over time.

For Nelnet, the model uses a Book Value of $102.74 per share and a Stable EPS of $4.90 per share, based on the median return on equity from the past 5 years. The Average Return on Equity is 5.40%, while the Cost of Equity is $10.24 per share. That gap produces an Excess Return estimate of $5.35 per share in the wrong direction, which signals earnings that do not fully cover the model’s required return on equity capital.

The Stable Book Value used in the model is $90.68 per share, again anchored to the median book value from the past 5 years. Putting these inputs together, the Excess Returns model arrives at an intrinsic value estimate of about $22.87 per share, which is very low relative to the recent share price of US$135.39 and implies the stock is very expensive using this lens.

Result: OVERVALUED

For a profitable company like Nelnet, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it links what you pay directly to the earnings the business is currently generating per share. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are willing to pay today for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E often reflects what investors expect for future growth and how much risk they see in those earnings. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk usually point to a lower multiple.

Nelnet currently trades on a P/E of 11.34x, compared with the Consumer Finance industry average of 8.65x and a peer group average of 9.28x. data.title: Academy Sports Ongoing Sales And Cash Flow Strains Test Growth Story
data.description:
• Academy Sports and Outdoors (NasdaqGS:ASO) is reporting ongoing annual sales declines alongside weak same store performance.
  • The company is also seeing a reduced free cash flow margin, pointing to more capital intensive operations.
  • These pressures raise questions around product mix, customer engagement and the effectiveness of current management decisions.

Academy Sports and Outdoors, a large sporting goods and outdoor retailer, operates in a segment where shoppers can increasingly compare prices and assortment across big box chains and online platforms. For investors looking at NasdaqGS:ASO, the combination of softer sales trends and a thinner free cash flow margin suggests the core business is under pressure at the store level and in the cash generation profile. That mix can matter if the company wants flexibility for store refreshes, merchandising changes or e commerce investments.

Looking ahead, you may want to watch how the company talks about merchandise categories, store traffic and capital spending priorities in upcoming updates. Any shifts in promotion intensity, store format or inventory approach could be important signals for how NasdaqGS:ASO is responding to competitive pressures and potential changes in customer behavior.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Academy Sports and Outdoors by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Academy Sports and Outdoors.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Academy Sports and Outdoors following this latest news.

For existing and prospective shareholders, the 2.4% annual sales decline over the last three years and a 3.9 percentage point drop in free cash flow margin are clear signals that the core retail model is under pressure. Weak same-store performance suggests existing locations are not pulling their weight, which can limit the benefits of new-store openings and e-commerce growth. At the same time, a lower free cash flow margin indicates more cash is being tied up in inventory, store build-outs or other capital-heavy projects, just as competition from retailers like Dick’s Sporting Goods and big-box chains such as Walmart and Target keeps pricing tight. With Academy Sports and Outdoors preparing to report its fourth-quarter and fiscal 2025 results on 17 March 2026, this combination of softer sales and a more capital intensive profile is likely to be a key focus for investors assessing how resilient the current strategy really is.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Academy Sports and Outdoors Narrative

  • The recent pressure on sales and free cash flow directly tests the narrative that store expansion and e-commerce growth can support a stronger, more scalable retail model.
  • Weak same-store sales and a thinner free cash flow margin challenge expectations that private-label development, loyalty programs and supply-chain investments will support stable margins and customer growth.
  • The apparent increase in capital intensity and competition-driven promotions may not be fully reflected in the existing narrative’s assumptions around operating leverage and cost savings.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Academy Sports and Outdoors to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Ongoing same-store sales weakness raises the risk that new-store openings could dilute overall productivity if customer demand does not keep pace.
  • ⚠️ A 3.9 percentage point reduction in free cash flow margin suggests less financial flexibility for buybacks, dividends or reinvestment if capital needs stay elevated.
  • 🎁 Analysts highlight that Academy Sports and Outdoors is trading at what they view as good value compared to peers and the wider specialty retail industry.
  • 🎁 Forecast earnings growth of 6.39% per year, if achieved, could help offset current operational pressures and support future reinvestment.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching whether upcoming results show any stabilization in same-store sales and whether management outlines a plan to improve free cash flow, for example through tighter inventory management or moderated capital spending. Commentary around promotional activity, customer mix and competitive dynamics versus retailers like Dick’s Sporting Goods and Walmart will also be important for judging how sustainable Academy Sports and Outdoors’ current playbook is. The tone and detail on future store openings, e-commerce investments and potential cost savings will help you gauge whether today’s pressure is temporary or part of a longer trend.

data.description:

  • The stock recently closed at US$250.69, with returns of 24.3% over 30 days, 23.8% year to date, 15.7% over 1 year, 171.0% over 3 years and 123.1% over 5 years, which sets an interesting backdrop for any valuation check.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Garmin's position in consumer durables and how investors are weighing its product portfolio, balance sheet strength and competitive standing against that share price. Together, these themes help frame whether the recent returns reflect changing expectations about the business or simply a shift in risk appetite around the stock.
  • Our valuation model currently gives Garmin a value score of 0 out of 6. Next we will break down what that means across different valuation approaches, and finish by looking at a more complete way to think about what the stock could be worth.

Garmin scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Garmin Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes projected future cash flows, discounts them back to today using a required return, and adds them up to estimate what the entire company might be worth right now.

For Garmin, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flows in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.38b. Analysts provide detailed estimates for the next few years, then Simply Wall St extends those projections further out. By 2030, the forecast free cash flow used in the model is $2.34b, with a series of annual projections in between that are all discounted to today.

Adding those discounted amounts together gives an estimated intrinsic value of $232.82 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $250.69, the DCF outcome indicates that the stock is trading around 7.7% above that estimate, which falls into a reasonable band rather than an extreme gap.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Garmin is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Garmin.

Approach 2: Garmin Price vs Earnings (P/E)

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that support that price. Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk usually justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk often line up with a lower, more cautious P/E.

Garmin currently trades on a P/E of about 29.0x. That sits above the Consumer Durables industry average of around 13.2x and also above its peer group average of about 21.2x. On the surface, that points to a premium price tag compared with both the wider industry and more direct peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Garmin is 23.2x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a “normal” P/E might look like given the company’s earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics. Because it folds these factors into a single number, the Fair Ratio can give a more tailored reference point than a simple comparison with broad industry or peer averages.

Setting the current P/E of 29.0x against the Fair Ratio of 23.2x suggests Garmin’s shares are pricing in more optimistic conditions than that model implies.

Result: OVERVALUED

These are simply your own story about Garmin that connects what you believe about its future revenue, earnings and margins to a financial forecast and Fair Value. You can then compare that to today’s price so you can judge whether the stock looks attractive. All of this is available within an easy tool on Simply Wall St’s Community page that updates as news and earnings arrive. Narratives can range from a bullish Garmin view with a Fair Value of US$310.0 per share to a more cautious view at US$185.0 per share, with the consensus Narrative in between at US$235.25, showing how different investors can look at the same company and reach very different but clearly quantified conclusions.

For Garmin, however, we’ll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Garmin Narratives:

🐂 Garmin Bull Case

Fair Value in this bullish Narrative: US$310.00 per share

Implied discount to this Fair Value at the recent US$250.69 share price: about 19.1%

Revenue growth assumption used in this Narrative: 11.32% a year

  • Builds on analysts at the high end of the forecast range, who model higher revenue, slightly lower margins, and a higher future P/E multiple to support a Fair Value of US$310.0 and a bullish price target of US$285.0.
  • Flags real risks around smartphone substitution, heavy competition from large tech players, product concentration, pricing pressure, and regulation, and encourages you to test whether those bullish assumptions feel realistic.

🐻 Garmin Bear Case

Fair Value in this more cautious Narrative: US$235.25 per share

Implied premium to this Fair Value at the recent US$250.69 share price: about 6.6%

  • Leans on the analyst consensus view that Garmin can still grow earnings through premium wearables, services, and aviation, but with more moderate revenue growth and margins than the bullish case.
  • Uses a Fair Value of US$235.25 and a consensus price target of about US$213.83, which together suggest the current P/E and share price assume a relatively full outcome for execution.
  • Highlights risks tied to trade policy, Marine and Outdoor softness, higher operating expenses, and foreign currency swings, which could make it harder to justify a premium valuation if they persist.

Between these two Narratives you can see how different assumptions on growth, margins, and P/E lead to very different views of what Garmin might be worth. This is exactly the kind of tension you may want to understand before deciding how the stock fits your own portfolio.

data.title: Accenture Targets AI And Network Intelligence To Reshape Long Term Growth
data.description:
• Accenture (NYSE:ACN) has entered a multi year collaboration with Mistral AI to co develop enterprise grade AI solutions for clients.
  • The company has also announced an agreement to acquire Ookla, a network intelligence and connectivity insights provider.
  • These moves add AI modeling capabilities and network performance data to Accenture's existing technology and consulting services.

Accenture is making these moves at a time when its share price sits at $209.89, with a 6.7% gain over the past week, a 20.4% decline over 30 days, and a 19.3% decline year to date. Over the past year, the stock shows a 37.9% decline, and over 3 and 5 years, returns of a 16.1% decline and a 10.6% decline respectively, signaling a challenging stretch for NYSE:ACN holders.

For you as an investor, the Mistral AI partnership and planned Ookla acquisition highlight where Accenture is choosing to invest its time and capital. How effectively the company turns these AI and network intelligence capabilities into real world client projects and long term contracts will be an important area to watch as you assess the role of NYSE:ACN in a broader portfolio.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Accenture by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Accenture.

We've flagged 0 risks for Accenture. See which could impact your investment.

For you as a shareholder, the Mistral AI collaboration and planned Ookla purchase point to Accenture doubling down on two areas clients are already asking about a lot: production grade AI and reliable connectivity. Mistral AI gives Accenture access to a focused suite of large language models that can be tailored to regional rules, including in Europe, which may matter for clients weighing data privacy and regulatory concerns. At the same time, bringing in Ookla’s Speedtest, Downdetector, Ekahau and RootMetrics data sets could help Accenture advise telecom operators, hyperscalers and large enterprises on how AI-powered tools sit on top of real world network performance rather than ideal lab conditions.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Accenture Narrative

  • The Mistral AI deal lines up with the existing narrative that partnerships and GenAI focused investments can support future revenue from large scale enterprise modernization projects.
  • Relying more on external AI partners such as Mistral AI, on top of OpenAI and Anthropic, could intensify execution risk if clients compare pricing and capabilities across Accenture and peers like IBM or Deloitte and push harder on fees.
  • The potential impact of network intelligence from Ookla on Accenture’s delivery quality, compared with consulting peers such as Capgemini or Cognizant, is not fully reflected in the existing narrative detail.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Accenture to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Growing dependence on external AI models and network data partners could pressure margins if licensing costs increase faster than Accenture can reprice client work.
  • ⚠️ Integrating Ookla’s products and aligning multiple AI partnerships at scale adds operational complexity that could weigh on project delivery if not managed carefully.
  • 🎁 Accenture is flagged as trading at 38.4% below one fair value estimate and at what is described as good value relative to peers and its industry.
  • 🎁 The company is identified as having an attractive and reliable dividend near 3.11%, with earnings growth over the past 5 years and forecasts pointing to further earnings growth.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to track how quickly Accenture turns the Mistral AI tie up into named client wins, particularly in Europe where data rules are front of mind, and whether those projects scale beyond pilots. For the Ookla deal, watch for concrete examples of combined offerings, such as AI-powered network planning or customer experience analytics for telecom operators, and how often those show up in client announcements. It is also worth keeping an eye on commentary in upcoming earnings calls about pricing, margins and any signs that AI related work is replacing, rather than just adding to, more traditional consulting projects.
